subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Windmill Power When Malawi, an agricultural country in southern Africa long troubled by poverty, was struck by severe famine in 2002, a widespread lack of electricity and running water hampered its recovery. To lessen his own family's struggles, teenager William Kamkwamba decided to build a windmill to generate power. Others doubted the viability of his idea; Kamkwamba didn't have the finances to buy the proper supplies for such a project. However, Kamkwamba persevered. His first windmill, completed when he was just fourteen, was largely built out of scrap materials—including an old bicycle frame and a tractor fan—and generated enough power for only one light bulb. Kamkwamba then built a stronger windmill, which powered four bulbs. He went on to build several more windmills, including one for pumping water, allowing his family to use electrical appliances and to irrigate their crops. Kamkwamba's success garnered international attention and inspired similar projects. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: William Kamkwamba managed to build windmills from scrap materials and generate power for his family.
William Kamkwamba did not have the proper supplies to build a windmill, so he used scrap materials.
Many Malawians faced problems following a 2002 famine because they lacked electricity and running water.
correct_answer: William Kamkwamba managed to build windmills from scrap materials and generate power for his family.

options: It demonstrates the growing success of William Kamkwamba's windmills.

correct_answer: It demonstrates the growing success of William Kamkwamba's windmills.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Douglass and Garrison: Two Abolitionists Frederick Douglass, an African American who had escaped slavery, and William Lloyd Garrison, a white writer who had grown up in the northern free states of the US, shared an important commonality. They were regarded as among the most important abolitionists—people who wanted to end the practice of slavery—of their time. At first, they worked together in the fight against slavery. However, as the years passed, Douglass and Garrison came to develop fundamental differences in their opinions about abolition. While Garrison viewed the US Constitution as supporting slavery, Douglass voiced his disagreement with this interpretation. Douglass even argued that the US Constitution could be used as a tool to outlaw slavery. In addition, Garrison believed that the Union should be broken up into North and South. Douglass, however, did not want the South to secede from the Union—that is, the United States of America—as he feared that enslaved people would then become isolated in the South. Douglass and Garrison were never able to resolve their disagreements, nor did they ever recover their friendship. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Although Douglass and Garrison initially worked together, they later disagreed on fundamental issues relating to the fight against slavery.
Despite their early work together as abolitionists, Douglass and Garrison had different views on the US Constitution.
correct_answer: Although Douglass and Garrison initially worked together, they later disagreed on fundamental issues relating to the fight against slavery.

Douglass and Garrison were never able to resolve their disagreements, nor did they ever recover their friendship.
options: It explains the origin of two different views of the US Constitution.
It distinguishes between the two abolitionists' views of the US Constitution.
correct_answer: It distinguishes between the two abolitionists' views of the US Constitution.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. The Iron Mountain When British explorer Sir John Ross encountered Inuit Greenlanders wielding iron tools in 1818, he was mystified; after all, they did not have the technology to extract iron from ore. They told Ross that their iron source was a saviksoah—an 'iron mountain'—but refused to disclose its location. Scientists back in Britain partially solved the puzzle by analysing some of the tools and determining that they likely came from a meteorite. Subsequently, several other explorers attempted, and failed, to confirm the source's existence. In 1894, however, American explorer Robert Peary had better luck. The Inuits were now obtaining iron through trade; they no longer needed the iron mountain and were willing to reveal it. A guide led Peary to three meteorites, which are currently housed in New York's American Museum of Natural History. The most massive, called Ahnighito, weighs thirty-one tonnes and is the largest meteorite in any museum. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: In 1894, Robert Peary successfully located three massive meteorites in Greenland.
The Greenlanders' mysterious iron mountain was eventually revealed to be three large meteorites.
correct_answer: The Greenlanders' mysterious iron mountain was eventually revealed to be three large meteorites.

The most massive, called Ahnighito, weighs thirty-one tonnes and is the largest meteorite in any museum.
options: It contrasts the Inuits' story with the scientific explanation of the iron.
It confirms an initial hypothesis about the source of the iron.
correct_answer: It confirms an initial hypothesis about the source of the iron.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Type 1 and Type 2 Diabetes Diabetes is a disease in which the level of glucose in the blood is too high. Glucose—a sugar needed by all cells of the body for energy—is normally transported to the cells with the help of the hormone insulin, which is produced by the pancreas. In type 1 diabetes, which is partly genetically inherited, the pancreas cannot make enough insulin because its insulin-producing cells have been destroyed by the body's own immune system. Thus, glucose accumulates in the blood. In contrast, people with type 2 diabetes can produce insulin, but their bodies are unable to utilise it effectively. Type 2 diabetes may be caused by lifestyle factors, such as high consumption of sugar, in addition to genetics. Type 1 diabetes has a lower prevalence than type 2, accounting for just five to ten percent of diabetes cases. The two conditions also differ by age of onset. Type 1 diabetes typically develops during childhood, while type 2 most commonly occurs in mid- to late adulthood. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Type 1 and type 2 diabetes differ from each other in terms of cause, age of onset and prevalence.
Type 1 diabetes is much less common than type 2 diabetes, affecting only five to ten percent of diabetes patients.
In type 1 diabetes, the pancreas does not produce enough insulin to move glucose into the cells of the body.
correct_answer: In type 1 diabetes, the pancreas does not produce enough insulin to move glucose into the cells of the body.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Saving the Manatee The Florida manatee, a subspecies of the West Indian manatee and relative of the dugong, is a large ocean mammal found in the US state of Florida's rivers and coastal areas. Unfortunately, the state's population of manatees has dwindled significantly over the years. Although the manatee has no natural predators, humans have contributed to many manatee deaths, mainly through boat collisions. The loss of warm-water habitats has also hastened the manatee's decline. Aerial surveys performed in the early 1990s found an estimated 1,267 manatees in Florida. Because of conservation efforts by several organisations, however, the number of manatees has rebounded recently. The creation of manatee protection zones—areas that boaters must avoid or drive through more slowly—has been particularly effective. As a result of these efforts, more than 6,000 manatees are found in Florida today. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Thanks to conservation efforts, Florida manatee populations have increased.
If boats are allowed at all in manatee protection zones, they are required to go at slower speeds.
correct_answer: Thanks to conservation efforts, Florida manatee populations have increased.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: The main, or central, idea of the passage is Thanks to conservation efforts, Florida manatee populations have increased. How does the detail in bold develop the main idea? The Florida manatee, a subspecies of the West Indian manatee and relative of the dugong, is a large ocean mammal found in the US state of Florida's rivers and coastal areas. As a result of these efforts, more than 6,000 manatees are found in Florida today.
options: It anticipates a potential flaw in conservation strategy.
It highlights a successful conservation strategy.
correct_answer: It highlights a successful conservation strategy.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Sign Language on Martha's Vineyard While researching Martha's Vineyard, an island off the coast of the north-eastern United States, anthropologist Nora Groce made a remarkable discovery. She learned that from the 1600s until the early 1900s, almost all the residents of two towns on the island—West Tisbury and Chilmark—were fluent in sign language. During these years, the towns had an unusually high population of deaf people, due to a form of hereditary deafness that ran in many Martha's Vineyard families. Consequently, a bilingual society developed. The islanders didn't attend special sign language classes; they simply learned the language as they grew up. One of the most interesting aspects of the phenomenon was that, in the absence of language barriers, deafness was not perceived as a disability. As one Vineyarder explained to Groce, deaf people were seen as 'just like everyone else'. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: The high incidence of deafness on Martha's Vineyard led to the development of a bilingual society.
The large population of deaf people on Martha's Vineyard was due to hereditary deafness.
correct_answer: The high incidence of deafness on Martha's Vineyard led to the development of a bilingual society.

skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: The main, or central, idea of the passage is The high incidence of deafness on Martha's Vineyard led to the development of a bilingual society. How does the detail in bold develop the main idea? While researching Martha's Vineyard, an island off the coast of the north-eastern United States, anthropologist Nora Groce made a remarkable discovery. One of the most interesting aspects of the phenomenon was that, in the absence of language barriers, deafness was not perceived as a disability. As one Vineyarder explained to Groce, deaf people were seen as 'just like everyone else'.
options: It explains two different ways that one can learn sign language.
It illustrates how natural and widespread the use of sign language was on the island.
correct_answer: It illustrates how natural and widespread the use of sign language was on the island.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Maggots as Medicine While serving in World War I, surgeon William Baer noticed something curious: soldiers whose wounds were infested with maggots (the wormlike immature larval form of flies) recovered faster than those whose wounds were maggot-free. Although Baer was not the first military doctor to take note of this phenomenon, he was the first to apply his observations to the problem of non-healing wounds. In the 1920s, he began treating hospital patients' serious wounds with maggots. The maggots consumed dead tissue, effectively cleaning the wounds and helping the patients heal. While maggot therapy fell out of favour after bacteria-killing antibiotic medications were introduced, it has made a comeback in modern medicine. Many clinicians have found that the treatment can be effective for certain types of severe wounds that do not respond to antibiotics. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: William Baer, a military surgeon, observed the advantages of maggot-infested wounds while serving in World War I.
As noted by William Baer, some severe wounds can be effectively treated with maggots, which consume dead tissue.
correct_answer: As noted by William Baer, some severe wounds can be effectively treated with maggots, which consume dead tissue.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. AC vs DC In the 1880s, the War of the Currents began. Thomas Edison developed direct current, or DC, a type of electricity that flows in one direction. By 1887, DC was in use around the United States. It had some drawbacks, though. Converting it to lower or higher voltages was difficult; that is, the current's force couldn't be changed easily. Transmitted at the low voltage needed for use in homes and businesses, it lost power after travelling only one and a half kilometres. Meanwhile, Nikola Tesla was working with alternating current, or AC. AC reverses direction many times per second and can be changed to different voltages. At high voltage, it can be transmitted efficiently, travelling hundreds of kilometres with little energy loss. Because of its advantages, AC triumphed by the end of the nineteenth century. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: AC has distinct advantages over DC, so AC won the War of the Currents.
AC reverses direction and can be converted to lower or higher voltage.
correct_answer: AC reverses direction and can be converted to lower or higher voltage.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Lost and Found In September 2015, scientists working off the Alaskan coast made a surprising discovery. At the bottom of the ocean were parts of two whaling ships from the 1800s. Fifty to sixty ships were known to have been lost in that area. The team of scientists also found chains, anchors and pots for holding whale oil. Interestingly, warmer temperatures may have played a part in the discovery of the whaling ships. The planet's warmer temperatures have caused melting of ocean ice in areas near the poles. Because of warmer weather and less ice, the team could work well into the autumn. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: The warming of the planet may have made the discovery of lost whaling ships possible.
In the 1800s, it was common for whaling ships to become lost off the coast of Alaska.
The warming of the planet caused warmer weather and less sea ice to be present in Alaska.
correct_answer: The warming of the planet may have made the discovery of lost whaling ships possible.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. The Maracanazo In 1950, the last game of the football World Cup was played in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil. Nearly two hundred thousand people squeezed into Rio's Maracanã stadium. Many expected the home team of Brazil to win easily. In one of the greatest upsets in football history, however, Uruguay won 2–1. Football is important in Brazil, and many Brazilians felt that losing this game hurt their nation's pride. Thus, the game came to be called the Maracanazo, meaning 'the Maracanã blow'. The word is still used to mean any game lost to a visiting team. Losing the 1950 World Cup had a lasting effect on the people of Brazil. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Sports experts can make the wrong guess about which team will win.
The World Cup is important to football fans all around the world.
Brazilians were deeply upset by the loss of the 1950 World Cup.
correct_answer: Brazilians were deeply upset by the loss of the 1950 World Cup.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Nuts and Peanuts According to many studies, eating nuts may be good for your heart. Nuts contain good fats, vitamin E and fibre, all of which are heart-healthy. Some people may not buy nuts often because they are expensive. But there are many less expensive foods that have the same health benefits as nuts. Peanuts, for example, are also good for your heart. Despite their name, peanuts are not actually nuts. They're legumes, like beans. In one study, people who ate either true nuts or peanuts were less likely to die of heart disease than people who didn't. Other studies have also suggested that peanuts are good for the heart. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Although many people do not realise it, peanuts are not true nuts.
True nuts are good for your heart, but peanuts can offer you similar health benefits.
Nuts contain many nutrients that are good for your heart, but they can be expensive.
correct_answer: Option, Although many people do not realise it, peanuts are not true nuts.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Ocean Cleanup The Great Pacific Garbage Patch is a giant pile of rubbish in the Pacific Ocean. It contains mainly bits of plastic from bags, water bottles and cups. Each year, thousands of sea animals, birds and fish die from eating the plastic. A Dutch businessman named Boyan Slat thought of a way to deal with the rubbish problem. In 2013, Slat started planning the Ocean Cleanup project. The project uses a long floating fence to gather the rubbish. Natural water currents and winds help with this collection process, and then ships remove the rubbish. Ocean Cleanup could be a first step to a cleaner Pacific. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: The rubbish problem in the Pacific causes the deaths of thousands of sea animals every year.
The Ocean Cleanup project uses the ocean's natural currents to help collect rubbish.
The rubbish problem in the Pacific led Boyan Slat to come up with an answer.
correct_answer: The rubbish problem in the Pacific led Boyan Slat to come up with an answer.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Not Just for Kids Boomerangs are flat, curved wooden objects that can return to you if thrown properly. Kids around the world toss them around for fun. However, boomerangs have a strong place in history. Thousands of years ago, the first peoples of Australia and in other parts of the world developed them as important tools. These early boomerangs weren't designed to return to the thrower like the modern toy versions. Instead, they were often used for hunting. They may have had other uses as well. For example, there is evidence that they may have been used for fighting, starting fires and digging through sandy terrain. So, while some people might consider boomerangs to be a toy, they actually serve many valuable uses. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: A boomerang will return to a person who throws it.
Before they were used for fun, boomerangs were used as tools.
Boomerangs were used as toys and tools by ancient people.
correct_answer: Option, A boomerang will return to a person who throws it.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. The Hair Dryer The first hair dryer was invented in 1888 by a hairstylist named Alexandre Godefroy. His invention had to be hooked up to a heater. It sent heat through a pipe to a 'hat' on the person's head. Before Godefroy's invention, there was no easy way to dry hair. People had to sit next to a stove or fireplace or out in the sun. They didn't wash their hair often. After Godefroy's invention, people could dry their hair faster. By the early 1920s, hairstyles and hair-washing habits were changing. Godefroy's invention was a first step in making it easier to dry and style hair. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Alexandre Godefroy changed what people could do with their hair.
People didn't wash their hair very often before the hair dryer was invented.
Alexandre Godefroy's first hair dryer was quite difficult to use.
correct_answer: Alexandre Godefroy changed what people could do with their hair.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Unexpected Heroes Maria Dickin was a woman who strongly believed in the well-being of animals. In 1943, she established the Dickin Medal for Animal Bravery in the United Kingdom. This award honours animals that show great courage and help people during wartime. The first animals to receive the Dickin Medal were three pigeons. The birds served in the United Kingdom's Royal Air Force during World War II. They carried messages to help locate air crews that had crashed. Since it began, the Dickin Medal has been awarded over seventy times. Its winners include thirty-four dogs, thirty-two pigeons, four horses and a cat named Simon. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Maria Dickin worked hard in her life to help animals in need.
The Dickin Medal recognises animals that serve during times of war.
Many who serve during a war receive awards for their courage.
correct_answer: Option, Maria Dickin worked hard in her life to help animals in need.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Mayan Life The Maya were an ancient people who lived in what is now Central America. The Mayan world was made up of two very different classes. Kings and other noble people ruled at the top. Peasants, or common people, served the nobles and were often treated poorly. The nobles wore fancy feathers and jewels. They lived in huge stone palaces and didn't work. Peasants, on the other hand, worked very hard at farming. They wore simple clothing and lived in huts made of mud or stone. Life was much more pleasant for a noble than a peasant. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Mayan nobles had easier lives than Mayan peasants.
The Mayan world was made up of nobles and peasants.
Mayan peasants had simple huts to live in and wore plain clothing.
correct_answer: Mayan nobles had easier lives than Mayan peasants.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. A Fearless Explorer English explorer Henry Hudson made four voyages to find a northern route to Asia. Each time, thick ocean ice brought his trips to an end. Though he never found a northern route, Hudson still became famous. On his voyages, he explored the eastern waterways of North America. He also discovered what would become known as Hudson Bay in northeastern Canada. A river, bridges, schools and even towns now bear his name. His efforts helped light the way for more Europeans to settle in North America. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Henry Hudson never reached his goal, but he is still one of the best-known explorers.
Thick ice was the main reason that Henry Hudson failed to make it to Asia.
Henry Hudson tried four times to find a northern route to Asia but had no success.
correct_answer: Henry Hudson never reached his goal, but he is still one of the best-known explorers.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Not Your Average Snowman Every winter, thousands of people travel to Zao Onsen, Japan. Many visit to see the 'snow monsters' that appear there each year. Called juhyo in Japanese, the snow monsters are not monsters at all. They are evergreen trees that have been covered with a thick coating of ice and snow. The snow monsters form when intense storms bring heavy snowfall and freezing winds through the Zao Mountain Range. Over time, so much snow and ice cover the trees that they change into immense, curious shapes. Many of them are over six metres tall. The forest of giant, snowy creatures is a strange and wonderful sight. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Japan's 'snow monsters' are huge evergreen trees covered with ice and snow.
Zao Onsen, Japan, gets a lot of visitors every winter.
Severe winter storms cover the trees in Zao Onsen with blankets of snow every year.
correct_answer: Japan's 'snow monsters' are huge evergreen trees covered with ice and snow.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. A Star in Hollywood and Beyond Shirley Temple is most often thought of as a famous child actor, singer and dancer. Starring in many films, the talented little girl was known for her curly hair and charm. However, Temple later had an important role in the United States government. She held positions under several different US presidents. In 1969, she was asked to represent the United States at the United Nations. She was also sent to the country of Ghana to represent the US. In addition, Temple was the first woman to be in charge of welcoming foreign leaders to the United States. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Sometimes famous actors leave Hollywood so they can become more involved in American government.
Shirley Temple was an important figure in the entertainment industry and American government.
Shirley Temple was a child actor who was known for her talent as well as her charm.
correct_answer: Option, Sometimes famous actors leave Hollywood so they can become more involved in American government.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Antarctica: Not What You Think When people think of Antarctica, they think of snow and ice. When they think of deserts, they think of sun, heat and sand. Yet in spite of its cold temperatures, Antarctica is actually a desert. In fact, it is the largest desert on the planet. A desert is a very dry environment that receives less than twenty-five centimetres of precipitation yearly. On average, Antarctica gets less than five centimetres. Most of that precipitation is in the form of snow, but rain or ice can sometimes fall as well. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Even though Antarctica is cold and has snow, it is a desert.
Most precipitation in Antarctica is in the form of snow, though it can also get rain or ice.
correct_answer: Even though Antarctica is cold and has snow, it is a desert.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Hubble Trouble In 1990, the Hubble Space Telescope was positioned some 547 kilometres above Earth. It began taking pictures of planets and stars and sending them back to Earth. Soon a problem was discovered, though. The main mirror in the telescope was the wrong shape, so the pictures were fuzzy. Astronauts on the space shuttle Endeavour were sent to fix the telescope in space. In five space walks, the astronauts changed the telescope's camera and added another set of mirrors. Since then, the Hubble Telescope has been taking amazing, clear pictures of the solar system. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Astronauts fixed a problem with the Hubble Space Telescope in outer space.
The Hubble Space Telescope developed problems after it was positioned in space.
correct_answer: Astronauts fixed a problem with the Hubble Space Telescope in outer space.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Snowflake Man You may have heard people say that no two snowflakes are exactly alike. It was Wilson Bentley who first discovered this. Bentley was born in the US state of Vermont in 1865. When he was fifteen, he received a microscope for his birthday. The first things he looked at were snowflakes. He wanted to draw them, but the snowflakes melted before he could finish. At last, Bentley discovered that he could capture a picture of a snowflake by using a camera along with his microscope. He spent many hours working in the cold to develop his new technique. Finally, in 1885, he took his first successful picture of a snowflake. Bentley went on to take pictures of more than five thousand snowflakes. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Wilson Bentley successfully photographed snowflakes after a long process of trial and error.
When Wilson Bentley first tried to take pictures of snowflakes, he was unsuccessful.
correct_answer: Wilson Bentley successfully photographed snowflakes after a long process of trial and error.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Training for the Tour The Tour de France is one of the most famous bicycle competitions in the world. For twenty-one days, cyclists race through the streets and up the mountains of France. Training for the Tour de France is just as intense. Athletes must prepare for months ahead of time. Some ride their bikes for as many as twenty to thirty hours per week. When not cycling, they exercise and lift weights to build their strength and endurance. In addition, cyclists follow a strict diet to stay in peak condition. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Cyclists must work hard to prepare for the Tour de France.
The Tour de France is a race that takes place over several weeks.
correct_answer: Cyclists must work hard to prepare for the Tour de France.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. The Challenger Explosion The space shuttle Challenger was supposed to take off on 22 January 1986. Because of bad weather and other problems, though, the launch was put off for several days. When the new launch day arrived, the weather was colder than usual. Some scientists warned that parts of the shuttle might not work well at those temperatures. They were worried about rubber rings, called O-rings, used in the launching rockets. In spite of these warnings, however, Challenger lifted off at 11:39 A.M. on 28 January. Less than eighty seconds after liftoff, the shuttle exploded. Later it was found that the O-rings had indeed failed, leading to the explosion. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: The space shuttle Challenger exploded only seconds after it lifted off the ground.
Because O-rings on the Challenger didn't work correctly, the space shuttle exploded.
correct_answer: Because O-rings on the Challenger didn't work correctly, the space shuttle exploded.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Spiders on Board In 2015, scientists discovered that a type of East Asian spider had settled in the US state of Georgia. How did these spiders make their way to North America? Most likely, they crossed the ocean in ships. Worldwide trade has made it easy for insects and spiders to move to new places. Spiders usually make their new homes near the ports where they first come off ships. However, these spiders were found far from ports. How did they get there? There may have been spider eggs hidden on a ute carrying goods on interstate motorways. The baby spiders probably hatched after the shipment arrived. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Spiders often come off ships and settle in ports.
Trade between countries helps spiders move around the planet.
correct_answer: Trade between countries helps spiders move around the planet.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Georges Seurat French painter Georges Seurat was born in Paris in 1859. Seurat received training in art as a young man. He also developed an interest in the science behind art, especially how the eye sees colour. As a result of his interest in science, Seurat began to paint in a new style. Instead of mixing colours, he painted small dots, or points, of colour on the canvas. From a distance, the colours seemed to blend together. Seurat's new style came to be called pointillism. It can be seen in one of his best-known paintings, A Sunday on La Grande Jatte. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: The style of painting called pointillism combines science and art.
After beginning training as an artist, Georges Seurat became interested in the science of how the eye sees colour.
Georges Seurat's interest in science led him to develop a new style of painting.
correct_answer: Georges Seurat's interest in science led him to develop a new style of painting.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. Mules and Horses A mule is a cross between a donkey and a horse. Its father is a donkey, and its mother is a horse. Mules are about the same size as horses and look like horses in certain ways. However, mules are quite different from horses when it comes to life on a farm. Mules are stronger than horses and usually don't have as many health problems as horses do. Mules also live longer than horses and don't need as much food. They can also act as guard animals, protecting other animals on the farm. For farmers, mules are often a better choice than horses. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Mules and horses have several key traits in common.
Mules make better working farm animals than horses.
Horses may have health problems and need a lot of food.
correct_answer: Option, Mules and horses have several key traits in common.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. The Boys' War During the American Civil War, soldiers were supposed to be at least eighteen years old. Because of the great need for soldiers, however, both sides accepted younger people. Thousands of boys between thirteen and seventeen fought for the North and the South. Even boys as young as ten could be drummers or messengers. Drummers used different drum rolls to send messages on the battlefield. Messengers were fast runners who passed messages between officers. In fact, so many boys served in the American Civil War that it became known as 'The Boys' War'. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Many boys served in the American Civil War because both armies needed more help.
Ten-year-old boys served as messengers or drummers during the American Civil War.
Drummers and messengers played an important part in the American Civil War.
correct_answer: Many boys served in the American Civil War because both armies needed more help.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. The Water Cycle In the water cycle, water travels around the planet in a repeating set of steps. Water from the surface of the Earth goes into the air. For example, when the ocean is heated by the sun, some of the water evaporates. In other words, it turns into water vapour, a gas. The water vapour then rises into the sky. When water vapour starts to condense, or change to liquid water, clouds form. Next, when the clouds are full of water, tiny drops form. The drops fall to Earth as rain or snow. Over time, the water will evaporate again, continuing the cycle. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: The water cycle takes place all around us all the time.
Water moves around the Earth in a series of steps known as the water cycle.
When the sun's rays heat the ocean's water, the water turns into water vapour.
correct_answer: Option, The water cycle takes place all around us all the time.

subject: english
skill_name: Determine the main idea of a passage
question_text: Read the passage. The Lives of Chimpanzees Jane Goodall began studying chimpanzees in the 1960s. Before Goodall, scientists had not had much success at observing chimpanzees. The scientists had either scared the chimps or spent too little time with them. Louis Leakey, a respected scientist, felt that Goodall was the right person to study the animals. He knew that Goodall was gentle and patient enough to stay with the chimps for a long time. Living in the wild in Tanzania for two years, Goodall made important discoveries. She found that chimps use tools, eat meat and have a simple 'language'. Thanks to Goodall, the world has a much better understanding of these smart, interesting animals. What is the main, or central, idea of the passage?
options: Jane Goodall changed how people think of chimpanzees in the wild.
Jane Goodall discovered that chimpanzees use tools and have a simple language.
Thanks to Louis Leakey's faith in her, Jane Goodall was chosen to study chimpanzees in the wild for two years.
correct_answer: Jane Goodall changed how people think of chimpanzees in the wild.

subject: english
skill_name: Identify audience and purpose
question_text: Review the letter to the editor and then answer the question. As a parent, I'm concerned that students are buying soft drinks in the school cafeteria every day, which affects their ability to learn. Too much sugar and caffeine makes it challenging for our students to concentrate. In addition, soft drink consumption can contribute to long-term health problems such as obesity and diabetes. I am not suggesting that NSW government schools should dictate what students eat and drink, but I do believe our schools should promote and foster healthy lifestyles, including proper nutrition. Students should be allowed to bring their own soft drinks from home, but offering soft drinks in our cafeterias is unacceptable. What is the text's main purpose?
options: to inform parents of the effect of nutrition on their children's education
to convince readers that soft drinks should not be consumed in schools in NSW
to prove that the writer is taking a reasonable position
to convince readers that soft drinks shouldn't be sold in school cafeterias in NSW
correct_answer: to convince readers that soft drinks shouldn't be sold in school cafeterias in NSW

subject: english
skill_name: Identify audience and purpose

We the peoples of the United Nations are determined to save succeeding generations from the scourge of war . . . to reaffirm faith in fundamental human rights . . . to establish conditions under which justice and respect for the obligations arising from treaties and other sources of international law can be maintained, and to promote social progress and better standards of life in larger freedom. Accordingly, our respective Governments, through representatives assembled in the city of San Francisco, who have exhibited their full powers found to be in good and due form, have agreed to the present Charter of the United Nations. Excerpted from Charter of the United Nations Who is the primary audience?
options: member states of the United Nations

subject: english
skill_name: Analyse short stories: set 1
question_text: Read the story. Seen 1 Knock, knock. 2 The child who lives in the caravan opposite my own is at my door. Her hair is so blonde, it's almost as white as mine, and it's jerked into a ponytail. Purple glasses. A darting, cat-like quality to her movements. I remain, unconcerned, on the sofa with my book. She'll leave shortly, as she always does. 3 Knock, knock. 4 I suppose it's rude to ignore her like this, but in all likelihood, the child wants what I can't offer: a cliché. A comforting, grandmotherly type who bakes cookies and pats arms and . . . well, whatever else it is they do. Knock, knock. I realise I've read an entire paragraph without absorbing it. Irritated, I flick the page back. Knock, knock, knock! Now I slap my book down. The child wants a cliché? Fine. But she's not getting Grandma. She's getting Crone. I stride to the door, open it a crack and scowl, expecting her to back off. Instead, she slinks through the sliver of open doorway. Did I mention she was cat-like? Catgirl stalks over to the sofa and settles herself there, placing her lavender backpack just so. A voice calls, 'Celia, I can't see you!' 'I don't need to be seen, Mother!' Catgirl's tone is indignant, like how cats would sound if they could speak. 'What do you mean, you don't need to be seen?' I snap. 'You're nowhere near old enough for that luxury.' I yank the door wide so that Catgirl's mother, standing in her own doorway, can see straight through to my sofa. That's how close together these caravans are. I expect the mother to say something like, 'Catgirl, you come home this instant!' But she just appraises me, nods and shuts her door. My mouth is literally hanging open at this point. What does the woman think I am, a free babysitter? Folding my arms, I turn back to the scene in my living room. Catgirl, now clutching a notepad and pencil, gazes at me through her ludicrous eyewear. 'I have a few questions,' she snips, all business. 'Excuse me?' 'May I ask you a few questions, please.' 'What is this, some sort of school project?' She blinks. 'Yes. Exactly. A history project.' Great. I'm history. But five minutes of humouring her is certainly a fair price to pay for permanent privacy, so I lay down my terms. 'After this? That's it. No more banging on my door. No impromptu visits. Agreed?' Catgirl nods curtly and taps her pencil on the notepad, like she has nine other interviews to rush off to. 'Fine,' I say. 'Proceed.' She pounces. 'What was your former identity?' 'Excuse me?' 'Let me rephrase. Who were you before the war?' 'What war?' She squints at me like I'm under a microscope. 'I think you know,' she purrs. She pulls a book out of her backpack and presents it with a flourish. Spies of World War II: Hiding in Plain Sight. Wow. Guess I was wrong about the Grandma cliché. I start laughing so hard that I have to put my hand on the kitchen table to steady myself. And I realise it's been a long time since I laughed. And then I remember that it's July. No school, no homework. No projects. Poor Catgirl sits on my sofa and watches me with a dismayed face, the ridiculous book sliding off her lap. Catgirl the spy. Thinking she's caught a fellow spy. 'I was a baby during World War II,' I chortle. 'How old do you imagine I am?' Catgirl looks confused at the thought that I was ever a baby. She opens her mouth, but I'm on a roll. 'Before you investigate your next case,' I start, putting 'case' in air quotes, 'you might want to bone up on common sense.' Catgirl's eyes fill—oh, geez—and I drop my hands. She reaches for her backpack, but it topples, spilling notepads, pencils and another book onto the floor. The Loneliness One Dare Not Sound: The Life of Emily Dickinson. Wait, Dickinson? I watch her shoving everything back into the backpack, and now it's like I'm the one looking through the microscope. And I see her as she must actually be: a quirky, intelligent girl with a hopeless glut of imagination, stuck in a relentlessly lonely summer and creating a whole scenar
options: It demonstrates the narrator's increasing love and admiration for Celia.
It demonstrates the narrator's increasing understanding of Celia.
It demonstrates Celia's increasing maturity and understanding.
correct_answer: Option, It demonstrates the narrator's increasing love and admiration for Celia.

options: Both of them enjoy investigating other people.

subject: english
skill_name: Analyse short stories: set 1
question_text: Read the story. Time on Her Hands 1 'Espresso is so last week. I only drink NCB now.' Tom tosses his hair, then examines his imaginary manicure. 2 I laugh. His accent is a mash-up of British royalty and faux-Parisian, which is not at all how Ms Bely actually talks. But given how I've described her, I can't blame him for thinking so. 3 Mum makes herself some coffee. 'What's NCB?' 4 'Nitro cold brew,' I say, trying out the same accent my brother used. 'It's divine.' 'It's iced coffee charged up with nitrogen,' Tom explains in his regular voice. 'I tried it once. It's actually not bad.' But I'm not ready to drop the impersonation, partly because I can't remember the last time my brother and I joked around at the breakfast table. Plus, I'm pretty good at the accent. 'I always grab one after hot yoga,' I say. 'The juxtaposition of hot and cool is so thought-provoking.' Mum frowns. 'Ada, is she really that insufferable? I thought she was nice.' 'Anyone can be nice.' I shrug. 'What's insufferable is her shallowness. Her "problems". Her . . . meaninglessness.' 'Nobody's meaningless.' 'I don't know about that. Anyway, I just can't take people seriously who never do anything.' 'What does she do when you babysit?' Tom asks. This is new and amazing—Tom's interest in my life. Maybe he finally understands that I'm not a goofy, empty-headed kid anymore. I've changed a lot since he moved out. 'Yoga, salon, whatever,' I tell him, sticking my nose up the way I imagine Ms Bely does. 'Oh, she also hides out in her quote, unquote "studio".' I lean in conspiratorially. 'The boys call it the "mummy-time room".' Tom snorts, and I grin. Not goofily, I don't think. 'Well, she provides you with a pay cheque,' Mum says, pointing to the clock. Whoops, my shift is from nine to one. I grab my bag. 'See you guys when I return from the Posh Side.' 'Good luck,' Tom says. Raindrops splatter on my umbrella as I step off the bus; we'll have to stay inside today. I picture the three of us in the playroom, crafting and playing board games. This is a sweet summer gig, I have to admit. Aiden throws his arms around my waist as if it's been a year instead of just a day. 'I had a paste for breakfast!' I gently brush the crumbs off my mini-me's cheek. 'Pastry,' says Tony, his brother. 'Paste is for sticking. Like what Mummy uses for—' 'I know what paste is!' 'No, 'cause you said—' 'Paste, what a great idea!' I exclaim. 'That's what we'll do today. How about we do an art project?' They look at me, interest piqued. I've got good at this. Ms Bely dashes down the stairs. Peach sneakers, black yoga pants, black-and-grey scoop-neck shirt artfully layered over a peach tank top. 'How are you, Ada?' 'Good, how about you?' 'Great! I'll be back in a couple of hours.' She kisses the boys, leaving peach lipstick on their cheeks. 'Have fun, kids.' 'We're doing paste, Mummy,' Aiden says importantly. Tony and I exchange a smile. 'Sounds cool!' Ms Bely winks at me before she leaves. False eyelashes for the gym, wow. In the playroom, we pull coloured paper, scissors and glue out of the crafts box. 'Tony, honey, this glue is nearly finished. Is there more somewhere?' 'Why don't you ask me?' says Aiden. 'I know, too. Anyway, you said paste.' Aiden has scrambled up the climbing ladder that's bolted to the wall; he's eye-level with me now. 'Paste, Ada, right? Not glue.' 'Same thing,' Tony and I say together, which makes us both laugh. 'Let's try the cupboard,' Tony says, pulling my hand. The cupboard is stacked with storage bins. I pull down the one labelled papermaking. Tony and I spend about ten minutes carefully sorting through the bin. It's full of old magazines and junk mail, but no glue. 'We make homemade paper from this,' Tony says. 'Really? Wait, where's Aiden?' Tony shrugs, and I sigh. Sometimes Aiden hides under his bed. 'Come on, Tony, let's go and find him.' But he's nowhere in his bedroom. 'Aiden!' I call, walking through the house. Then louder. 'Aiden!' 'Aiden! It's not funny!'

subject: english
skill_name: Analyse short stories: set 1
question_text: Read the story. Dark Day 1 Mercy stirred the cornmeal mush, standard breakfast fare at Seely's Tavern. Nearby, her mother plucked a chicken for the midday stew. Their silence was companionable; neither was inclined towards conversation for its own sake, particularly this early. Soon enough, the tavern would echo with scraping, chewing, clattering and, most likely, arguing. For now, they both relished the quiet. 2 When the mush was butter-smooth, Mercy rose from the hearth and lifted the egg basket from its hook. 3 'Best wait,' Mother said. With Mr Seely not yet returned from trading, she was more vigilant than usual. 'It's full morning,' Mercy responded. 'The fog makes it look darker than it is.' Mrs Seely pressed her lips together but nodded, and Mercy stepped into the gloomy morning. Once in the coop, she tossed grain to the chickens, then pulled her poem from her apron pocket for yet another read. Entitled 'Appeal', it was a direct address to the odd weather of late: Mornings dark with heavy fog, skies so pale and sallow! Why the strange and reddened sun, upon this land so fallow? Shall you steal away our blue? Farewell, blue sky and bluebell? Save it for the Patriots' coats? Must even you rebel? Mercy frowned; it was terrible. She should throw it to the chickens. Absolutely, she could never show this drivel to John. Not that John was such a graceful poet, but Mercy didn't want him to think she wasn't. She had met him last week, when he passed through Dole with the rest of his company. They'd taken their midday meal at the tavern, all of them dressed in their tattered Continental Army uniforms. As usual, the conversation at the table was insufferable, with the men braying like donkeys about freedom and slaughter. When Mr Seely ventured that perhaps even freedom wasn't worth such bloodshed, John puffed out his chest and proclaimed, 'No redcoat could spill my blood!' 'How can one not comprehend one's own vulnerability?' Mercy muttered, gathering the wooden bowls. She was speaking to herself, but John had heard, and his eyes met hers. She took the bowls and fled. He found her later, when she was banking up the hearth. 'Begging your pardon, miss,' he said. His face was shy, the bluster gone. He handed her a folded paper, touched his hat and left. The note contained three scribbled lines. Perhaps young men's braggadocio is nothing more Than nature's last defence against tyranny. Else there'd be no war. — John Petty, 1780 Maybe not a poet, but not a thoughtless rooster, either. Later, Mercy placed his note between the pages of one of her old storybooks. That's when the idea for 'Appeal' came to her. She was grateful; it had been a while since she'd had the urge to write poetry. If she could improve 'Appeal' to the point that it no longer disgusted her, she'd slip it to John when his company passed back through. If they passed back through. Against her will, Mercy pictured muskets and cannons. She shuddered, stuffing the poem back into her pocket. Most likely, she'd never see John again. Not in this bleak world, a world that didn't even deserve poetry. Regardless, she'd tarried too long. Quickly, she gathered the eggs, flung open the door—and blanched. The sky appeared darker than when she'd entered the coop. Had her dark thoughts afflicted her vision? Mercy shook her head; what a notion! That's what came of stuffing her head with useless poetry. A storm was coming, that was all. She carried the eggs to the kitchen, then joined her mother in serving the mush. They bade goodbye to the travellers, who were anxious to leave before the storm. It grew ever darker as mother and daughter attended to their other chores. And yet, there was no thunder, no rain. At last, they could not deny that something was wrong. Mercy and Mrs Seely lit candles, then bowed their heads. 'Be steady,' Mother whispered. 'Yes,' Mercy whispered back. By noon, it was as black as night. Villagers trickled into the tavern, wanting to know whether the whole state o
options: It explains why Mercy later considers giving up poetry.
It shows that the villagers think the weather is connected to the war.
It describes the unusual weather conditions preceding the Dark Day.
correct_answer: It describes the unusual weather conditions preceding the Dark Day.

Absolutely, she could never show this drivel to John. Not that John was such a graceful poet, but Mercy didn't want him to think she wasn't. She had met him last week, when he passed through Dole with the rest of his company. They'd taken their midday meal at the tavern, all of them dressed in their tattered Continental Army uniforms. As usual, the conversation at the table was insufferable, with the men braying like donkeys about freedom and slaughter. When Mr Seely ventured that perhaps even freedom wasn't worth such bloodshed, John puffed out his chest and proclaimed, 'No redcoat could spill my blood!' 'How can one not comprehend one's own vulnerability?' Mercy muttered, gathering the wooden bowls. She was speaking to herself, but John had heard, and his eyes met hers. She took the bowls and fled. He found her later, when she was banking up the hearth. 'Begging your pardon, miss,' he said. His face was shy, the bluster gone. He handed her a folded paper, touched his hat and left. The note contained three scribbled lines. Perhaps young men's braggadocio is nothing more Than nature's last defence against tyranny. Else there'd be no war. — John Petty, 1780 Maybe not a poet, but not a thoughtless rooster, either. Later, Mercy placed his note between the pages of one of her old storybooks. That's when the idea for 'Appeal' came to her. She was grateful; it had been a while since she'd had the urge to write poetry. If she could improve 'Appeal' to the point that it no longer disgusted her, she'd slip it to John when his company passed back through. If they passed back through. Against her will, Mercy pictured muskets and cannons. She shuddered, stuffing the poem back into her pocket. Most likely, she'd never see John again. Not in this bleak world, a world that didn't even deserve poetry. Regardless, she'd tarried too long. Quickly, she gathered the eggs, flung open the door—and blanched. The sky appeared darker than when she'd entered the coop. Had her dark thoughts afflicted her vision? Mercy shook her head; what a notion! That's what came of stuffing her head with useless poetry. A storm was coming, that was all. She carried the eggs to the kitchen, then joined her mother in serving the mush. They bade goodbye to the travellers, who were anxious to leave before the storm. It grew ever darker as mother and daughter attended to their other chores. And yet, there was no thunder, no rain. At last, they could not deny that something was wrong. Mercy and Mrs Seely lit candles, then bowed their heads. 'Be steady,' Mother whispered. 'Yes,' Mercy whispered back. By noon, it was as black as night. Villagers trickled into
options: She is reminded of the beauty in the world, in spite of the conflicts around them.
She wants to give her poem to Mr Daly and Mrs Thomas.
She worries that she won't be able to write another poem before John returns.
correct_answer: She is reminded of the beauty in the world, in spite of the conflicts around them.

subject: english
skill_name: Analyse short stories: set 1
question_text: Read the story. The Burden of Choice 1 As he parks, my father says, 'Leah. About Madison. She's not exactly . . . herself.' 2 I busy myself with the door handle. 'Yeah. I mean, hard place to be. The hospital.' 3 'And also, when she comes home. The adjusting.' 4 'Right.' 5 My father and I both tend to speak in staccato when we're nervous. We walk towards the medical centre, and I consider his phrasing: 'Not exactly herself.' I wonder if she even cares. Maybe she's happy to cast off her old self. To moult. Like that time at the Turvy Street Festival. We'd gone together, just the two of us. Booths full of pottery and batik and beaded jewellery. The smell of sugar and grease. We had just split some fairy floss, and Madison had gone to throw away the stick. She came running back. 'Leah, there's a free haircut booth!' 'Nothing's free,' I said. She laughed, grabbed my hand and pointed to a plastic banner: Free Cuts from À L'envers Salon! The bouncing hairdresser spied us immediately and cried, 'Ladies! Let Pierre transform you!' 'Let's go,' I muttered, but Madison was already jumping into the chair and requesting 'something radical'. Pierre's scissors flashed; his razor whirred. The two of them chatted like soulmates. Fifteen minutes later, he triumphantly spun her chair around to face the mirror. She stared at her reflection. 'I'm gone,' she breathed. I shuddered, but Not-Madison didn't notice. She flung her arms around Pierre, then plopped a ten-dollar tip into his hand. The last of her money. Now I shudder again, and my father touches my shoulder. 'I mean, why would she have thought it was a good idea to—' I cut myself off. My father says, 'This might not be the best time—' 'I know. Forget it.' And suddenly, we're in the lobby, the lifts yawning open. I stop walking. 'We should get a coffee,' I say. 'For Mum. Shouldn't we?' Dad nods. 'All right.' We turn from the lifts. The cafeteria: salads in cling film, chips wilting under heat lamps, aluminium vats of coffee. Dad and I deliberate over caffeine, sweetener, dairy. There are two lines for the cashier, one on either side of her. She takes turns so that the lines will stay even, but they aren't. I make sure we join the longer one, my father holding one cup of regular and one cup of decaf, me clutching packets of sugar and sweetener. So many choices. 'Just choose!' I can hear Madison saying. And I remember plastic yellow ducks floating in a tub of greasy water, carnival music, my parents laughing. Ten years ago? Twelve? We were playing one of those rigged funfair games—the one where you get to catch a duck with a fishing rod, then exchange it for a prize. Madison caught her duck within three seconds. The booth worker unhooked it, flicked his eyes over the label on the bottom and shouted, 'Goldilocks here just won herself a spider ring!' Madison was so proud of that ring, twisting it around her index finger and grinning. But I didn't want a dud prize. I wanted the plushy purple dog that no one ever won. I stared at the revolving ducks. One of them was right, but which? My hands were sweating as I gripped the rod. Did I ever even choose? I don't remember. I only remember Madison jumping up and down, yelling, 'Choose! Choose! Leah, just choose!' The lift dings, and my father and I step out, carefully balancing our coffee collection. We walk down the hall, past the nurses' station, past closed door after closed door, until finally my father stops at Madison's. 'Dad,' I say. He turns. 'I need the toilet. Here.' I dump the packets of sugar and sweetener into his jacket pocket. I don't even open the door for him. The toilets: banging doors, mirrors under unforgiving lights. I stare at the circles under my eyes. I could text him: Not feeling well. Will wait for you in lobby, no, in front of hospital, no, in car park, no, at home inside a wardrobe. I found Madison there once, in the wardrobe in the study. About five years ago. It was the day she brought Impulse home—mangy, scrawny, abandoned
options: It illustrates Madison's and Leah's different attitudes towards change.
It illustrates the reason for Madison and Leah's arguments.
correct_answer: It illustrates Madison's and Leah's different attitudes towards change.

Leah is afraid of making the wrong choice.
Leah doesn't want to appear as impatient as Madison.

subject: english
skill_name: Analyse short stories: set 1
question_text: Read the story. Mosaic 1 A throng of passengers got off at the Division Street stop, so Masha and Molly were finally able to sit. 2 'Ah, that's better,' Molly said. 'Now we can talk. Masha, come to the presentation tonight. Please. It's not a social thing. It's educational!' 3 The tram swayed, and Masha grabbed Molly's arm to steady herself. 'I already told you,' she said. 'I can't.' 4 'Can't!' Molly waved her hands as if batting away a fly. 'Of course you can. No work tomorrow, remember? Relax for once! Do something fun.' 'I am. Mama and I are baking rugelach.' Seeing Molly's confused expression, Masha explained, 'It's a dessert.' Molly waved her hand again. 'Rosie can help her.' 'Rosie!' Masha laughed. 'Why not? I bet you made roo-gla at her age.' 'Rosie isn't interested. She's Australian. And it's rugelach, not "roo-gla". You make it sound like a dance.' 'See, now that would be fun.' Molly deepened her voice. 'Ladies and gentlemen! Presenting the roo-gla, the hottest dance of 1920!' She giggled. 'Anyway, what are you even saying? You're Australian, too. You live here, don't you?' 'Yes, but Rosie is . . . of here.' Masha's younger sister wore sailor-style blouses, owned a dozen ribbons for her bobbed hair and knew all the latest songs. The heady aroma of yeast and nuts could never transport her back to their homeland, which Rosie barely remembered. As far as Rosie was concerned, Australia was their homeland. Whoops, Molly was talking. '—just as Australian as she is. As Australian as lamingtons! Come with me, Masha, and I'll make some lamingtons for your mum. You don't need roo-gla.' Molly paused. 'Ru-ge-lach.' Masha fell back against the seat, laughing. But then she sat up. 'Wait a minute, Molly. Why are you so keen on this lecture?' 'I'm very interested in ancient art,' Molly said. She tossed her copper curls and gazed at Masha with wide-eyed innocence. Masha stared back, eyes narrowed. Finally, Molly threw up her hands. 'I overheard Stephen talking, and—' 'Aha! So Stephen will be there, will he?' Masha tossed her own hair in an imitation of Molly. 'I'm very interested in ancient art,' she squeaked. They collapsed into laughter. 'Okay, okay.' Molly wiped her eyes. 'Now will you come? Who knows, maybe you'll meet a young man!' She elbowed Masha. 'Someone you can bake roo-gla for.' 'Don't be silly. The last thing I want is to spend my life cooking for some husband.' 'See! You're more Australian than you think!' It was true. If Masha ever returned to the old country, she wouldn't fit in. Nor, she realised suddenly, would she want to. And yet, she didn't want to wear a bob either. Or eat lamingtons. She wanted rugelach. And borscht. And to sing the old folk songs to her children. If, that is, she remembered them by the time she had children. She rubbed her temples. 'What's wrong?' Molly asked. 'Do you remember Ireland?' 'Not much, no. Do you remember Poland?' 'Just fragments,' Masha said. 'I don't want to let go of them.' 'Ah.' There was understanding in Molly's eyes. 'They're part of you, those fragments.' 'Yes. Yes, exactly.' Masha squeezed Molly's hand as the tram swayed again. Despite all their differences, Molly was the best friend Masha had ever had. Molly understood her. 'Okay, Molly. I'll come with you,' Masha said. 'Oh, Masha, thank you! And you know what, I bet you'll like it more than I do!' Masha shook her head, already missing the smell of the rugelach dough. She wondered whether her mother would mind. But Mama was thrilled. 'Wonderful, Masha, you need to go out more!' Rosie came running. 'Masha, try my blue dress. And let me put some rouge on your cheeks. No, don't rub it off. Honestly!' When Molly came by, she pretended to fall over. 'Look who's modern now! Seriously, Masha, you're beautiful.' As they walked arm in arm up Unity Street, Masha did feel relaxed. Maybe it was a good thing, getting out sometimes. When they stepped into the Alliance lobby, Masha gazed around in amazement. 'Wait until you see the lectur

Presenting the roo-gla, the hottest dance of 1920!' She giggled. 'Anyway, what are you even saying? You're Australian, too. You live here, don't you?' 'Yes, but Rosie is . . . of here.' Masha's younger sister wore sailor-style blouses, owned a dozen ribbons for her bobbed hair and knew all the latest songs. The heady aroma of yeast and nuts could never transport her back to their homeland, which Rosie barely remembered. As far as Rosie was concerned, Australia was their homeland. Whoops, Molly was talking. '—just as Australian as she is. As Australian as lamingtons! Come with me, Masha, and I'll make some lamingtons for your mum. You don't need roo-gla.' Molly paused. 'Ru-ge-lach.' Masha fell back against the seat, laughing. But then she sat up. 'Wait a minute, Molly. Why are you so keen on this lecture?' 'I'm very interested in ancient art,' Molly said. She tossed her copper curls and gazed at Masha with wide-eyed innocence. Masha stared back, eyes narrowed. Finally, Molly threw up her hands. 'I overheard Stephen talking, and—' 'Aha! So Stephen will be there, will he?' Masha tossed her own hair in an imitation of Molly. 'I'm very interested in ancient art,' she squeaked. They collapsed into laughter. 'Okay, okay.' Molly wiped her eyes. 'Now will you come? Who knows, maybe you'll meet a young man!' She elbowed Masha. 'Someone you can bake roo-gla for.' 'Don't be silly. The last thing I want is to spend my life cooking for some husband.' 'See! You're more Australian than you think!' It was true. If Masha ever returned to the old country, she wouldn't fit in. Nor, she realised suddenly, would she want to. And yet, she didn't want to wear a bob either. Or eat lamingtons. She wanted rugelach. And borscht. And to sing the old folk songs to her children. If, that is, she remembered them by the time she had children. She rubbed her temples. 'What's wrong?' Molly asked. 'Do you remember Ireland?' 'Not much, no. Do you remember Poland?' 'Just fragments,' Masha said. 'I don't want to let go of them.' 'Ah.' There was understanding in Molly's eyes. 'They're part of you, those fragments.' 'Yes. Yes, exactly.' Masha squeezed Molly's hand as the tram swayed again. Despite all their differences, Molly was the best friend Masha had ever had. Molly understood her. 'Okay, Molly. I'll come with you,' Masha said. 'Oh, Masha, thank you! And you know what, I bet you'll like it more than I do!' Masha shook her head, already missing the smell of the rugelach dough. She wondered whether her mother would mind. But Mama was thrilled. 'Wonderful, Masha, you need to go out more!' Rosie came running. 'Masha, try my blue dress. And let me put some rouge on your cheeks. No, don't rub it off. Honestly!' When Molly came by, she pretended to fall over. 'Look who's modern now! Seriously, Masha, you're beautiful.' As they walked arm in arm up Unity Street, Masha did feel relaxed. Maybe it was a good thing, getting out sometimes. When they stepped into the Alliance lobby, Masha gazed
options: She realises she can create mosaics to connect with her heritage.
She realises that her 'fragmented' identity is a thing to be appreciated.
She was able to help Molly and Stephen connect.
correct_answer: Option, She realises she can create mosaics to connect with her heritage.

subject: english
skill_name: Analyse short stories: set 1
question_text: Read the story. A Roof, a Palace 1 After Brett gave Shelton the 'grand tour', they sat at Shelton's new kitchen table for the last of the paperwork. Shelton wished he could brew them some tea, but he didn't have tea bags yet. Next time. 2 As he signed the forms, he imagined himself retrieving the tea bags from the storage bin as water boiled on his camping stove. The image filled him with happiness. 3 Brett stacked the paperwork into his briefcase. 'I'll just put this in the car. Be right back.' When he returned, he was carrying two presents. Embarrassed, Shelton said, 'Aw, you didn't need—' 'It's your housewarming!' Brett was grinning like a kid. 'Open the gold one first.' Shelton smiled and carefully removed the paper. The gift was an official ROOF toolbox. As usual, Shelton grimaced inwardly at the lopsided logo. He would love to clean it up a little, fix the perspective. He had been known for his logos, once upon a time. He opened the toolbox; it was fully stocked. 'This is incredible. Thank you.' 'And this one's just from me.' Brett nudged the smaller gift. 'It's okay, we're allowed,' he added quickly. 'Discretionary fund.' Shelton took his time with this wrapping paper, too. It was beautiful—thick and cream coloured. When at last he uncovered the sketch pad and pens, his eyes filled. How long since he'd used anything besides recycled scraps and stubby library pencils? 'Thank you,' he whispered. 'Thought you'd like it.' Brett beamed and stood up. 'I'll let you settle in. See you tomorrow for the interview.' Shelton nodded. He wasn't thrilled about being on television, but he owed them one. He closed the door behind Brett and turned the deadbolt: another thrill. Security was a luxury he'd once taken for granted. Never again. He looked around, breathing deeply. If there was a heaven, he was in it. He removed his shoes, lay down on the daybed and watched the late-afternoon sunlight move across the wall. Then he fell into the deepest sleep he'd had in what seemed like forever. The next morning, he kept himself busy with a million little chores: polishing his lantern, organising his belongings, buffing the floor with an old T-shirt. By the time Brett and the news crew arrived, the whole place sparkled. 'Nice digs,' the cameraperson said as she set up battery-powered lights. 'Absolutely,' enthused the reporter. He wore an expensive-looking windbreaker over a collared shirt—the same look Shelton himself had once favoured. He gave Shelton and Brett a few interviewing tips and showed them where to stand. 'Remember—look at me, not the camera,' he said. At the cameraperson's signal, the reporter spoke importantly into his microphone. 'As many are all too aware, Semblance City has seen a marked increase in homelessness. ROOF—the Realistic Occupancy Options Fund—offers a unique solution: tiny homes for the homeless. I'm here with Shelton Pova, ROOF's newest occupant, and his case manager, Brett Green. Mr Pova, congratulations.' 'Thank you.' The reporter's face turned sombre. 'You were homeless for quite a while. Were you sleeping on the streets?' 'I . . . it varied. At first, I lived in my car. After I had to give that up, I used the shelter, and yes, sometimes the street. It was a back-and-forth thing.' 'And Mr Green, you recruited Mr Pova for ROOF, correct?' 'That's right. He was a perfect candidate.' 'It must be wonderful to have a roof over your head, Mr Pova. In fact, Mr Green, these tiny homes—there are eighteen in the cluster—are little more than roofs. No electricity, no kitchen, no bathrooms?' 'There's a communal—' 'An outdoor shower and a toilet block. How would you respond to critics who call this a campground?' A campground? Shelton looked around, trying to see his home as the reporter probably saw it. As he himself would once have seen it. He smiled a little, remembering a picture from his old childhood book of optical illusions. It showed a young man, but if you looked at it a certain way, you saw an old man instead. She
options: Shelton aspires to be a professional artist.
Shelton had once been a successful artist.
Shelton took up drawing as a hobby when he was homeless.
correct_answer: Option, Shelton aspires to be a professional artist.

subject: english
skill_name: Analyse short stories: set 2
question_text: Read the story. The Rat Within 1 A crust of bread slid among the dirt and debris on the floor. The rat, quivering under the darkest bunk, hoped the bread would come his way. But the steamship heaved violently, and everything—the debris, the dirt, the crust of bread—slid in the opposite direction. The rat skittered for purchase on the rotting planks. Then the ship heaved again, and everything slid towards the rat. Then away. Then towards. Then away. Then towards. Away. Towards. Away, towards, away, towards, away, towards, away. The crust disappeared from sight, and the rat's belly remained a cavern of need. 2 The rat listened to the humans groan. With the hatches battened down against the rough weather, the humans couldn't go out on deck. They had to stuff themselves into the berths and live alongside the rats. Most lay flat in their narrow bunks, some silent, others retching, a few jabbering fevered nonsense. At least in their weakened condition, the humans weren't so dangerous. Up in the first-class cabins, they were lethal. The rat would never go up there. Rats who ascended to the higher classes didn't return, and it wasn't because they were dining on fruit and cheese. No. This rat would take steerage crumbs over first-class poison any day. 'Ah, steerage, such wonderful accommodations. If you're a rat,' muttered a voice. Startled, the rat looked up and found himself staring straight into a feverish eye. A human was hanging his head over the edge of the bunk. Could the eye see him in the gloom? 'You'd think that for sixty rubles a ticket, we'd get breathable air. Is this how your America is? One big scam?' He coughed, then turned contrite. 'Ah, excuse me, I'm not well. America's a fine country, a fine country. The golden land!' He dropped a careless hand in front of the rat, his fingers within a millimetre of the rat's teeth. The rat wondered whether perhaps the human was delirious, his brain addled by fever. 'Everything golden and new,' the human continued dreamily. 'Even the century, eh? Nineteen hundred, so new I keep forgetting it's here. Golden time, golden country.' Well. The rat had never been to America, but he knew it wasn't golden. Was this human an idiot? 'I'm not an idiot,' the human sighed. 'It's not real gold; it's opportunity. Where I'm from, there isn't any. In America, there are barrels. Enough even for a rat.' The human wheezed a laugh. Had the human read his mind? The rat began to wonder whether perhaps it was he, not the human, who was delirious. Maybe he had let himself get too hungry. 'You're weak,' said the human. 'You need food.' He rustled in his bunk, then offered the rat a hunk of bread. It smelled odd, though. Poison? 'It's just Mutter's vinegar. Prevents mould. Go ahead. I'm not malicious. Though I'll concede I'm delirious. I've been talking out of my head, seeing things that aren't there. You, for instance. I think I know what you are. And it's no rat. So eat. You'll do us both a favour.' The human laughed again, then coughed. The rat ate, and the human smiled wanly. 'Fruit and cheese it's not, but it fills the cavern.' It certainly did. Satiated, the rat closed his eyes, but the human rudely nudged his nose. The rat ignored him, burrowing into himself. He needed sleep. This absurd human, though—he wouldn't let up. Shmuel, that was the human's name. The rat remembered now. '"If you want your dreams to come true, don't sleep,"' Shmuel whispered. Ah yes, his favourite saying. The human coughed. 'Get up, Shmuel. We have to go to the upper deck. Clear out the lungs.' This was a crazy idea. Meshuggah, as they said back home. Did this ridiculous, delirious Shmuel think they could just breeze around first-class? Their ticket was for steerage. But now Shmuel was holding the rat. 'We're only stealing air, which no one should have to steal,' he muttered. 'You know what could happen if we don't, Shmuel.' The human was right. If the lungs were bad, they wouldn't be allowed to live in America. They'd be sent
options: It recalls the unpleasant rocking motion of the steamship.
It suggests that the voyage is both pleasant and unpleasant.
It emphasises that the long voyage has only just begun.
It evokes a sense of relaxation in the reader.
correct_answer: It recalls the unpleasant rocking motion of the steamship.

Up in the first-class cabins, they were lethal. The rat would never go up there. Rats who ascended to the higher classes didn't return, and it wasn't because they were dining on fruit and cheese. No. This rat would take steerage crumbs over first-class poison any day. 'Ah, steerage, such wonderful accommodations. If you're a rat,' muttered a voice. Startled, the rat looked up and found himself staring straight into a feverish eye. A human was hanging his head over the edge of the bunk. Could the eye see him in the gloom? 'You'd think that for sixty rubles a ticket, we'd get breathable air. Is this how your America is? One big scam?' He coughed, then turned contrite. 'Ah, excuse me, I'm not well. America's a fine country, a fine country. The golden land!' He dropped a careless hand in front of the rat, his fingers within a millimetre of the rat's teeth. The rat wondered whether perhaps the human was delirious, his brain addled by fever. 'Everything golden and new,' the human continued dreamily. 'Even the century, eh? Nineteen hundred, so new I keep forgetting it's here. Golden time, golden country.' Well. The rat had never been to America, but he knew it wasn't golden. Was this human an idiot? 'I'm not an idiot,' the human sighed. 'It's not real gold; it's opportunity. Where I'm from, there isn't any. In America, there are barrels. Enough even for a rat.' The human wheezed a laugh. Had the human read his mind? The rat began to wonder whether perhaps it was he, not the human, who was delirious. Maybe he had let himself get too hungry. 'You're weak,' said the human. 'You need food.' He rustled in his bunk, then offered the rat a hunk of bread. It smelled odd, though. Poison? 'It's just Mutter's vinegar. Prevents mould. Go ahead. I'm not malicious. Though I'll concede I'm delirious. I've been talking out of my head, seeing things that aren't there. You, for instance. I think I know what you are. And it's no rat. So eat. You'll do us both a favour.' The human laughed again, then coughed. The rat ate, and the human smiled wanly. 'Fruit and cheese it's not, but it fills the cavern.' It certainly did. Satiated, the rat closed his eyes, but the human rudely nudged his nose. The rat ignored him, burrowing into himself. He needed sleep. This absurd human, though—he wouldn't let up. Shmuel, that was the human's name. The rat remembered now. '"If you want your dreams to come true, don't sleep,"' Shmuel whispered. Ah yes, his favourite saying. The human coughed. 'Get up, Shmuel. We have to go to the upper deck. Clear out the lungs.' This was a crazy idea. Meshuggah, as they said back home. Did this ridiculous, delirious Shmuel think they could just breeze around first-class? Their ticket was for steerage. But now Shmuel was holding the rat. 'We're only stealing air, which no one should have to steal,' he muttered. 'You know what could happen if we don't, Shmuel.' The human was right. If the lungs were bad, they wouldn't
options: Shmuel is hallucinating that he is the rat.
Shmuel is afraid the rat will steal his food.
Shmuel is making friends with the rat.
correct_answer: Shmuel is hallucinating that he is the rat.
